text.content: A Review of Self-regulated Learning: Six Models and Four Directions for Research

title.plain_text: A Review of Self-regulated Learning: Six Models and Four Directions for Research

blocks.text: Abstract

text.content: Abstract

rich_text.plain_text: Abstract

blocks.text: The paper discusses 6 of the most well known models of SRL. They present differences in history, description of the model, empirical support and available instruments that stem from differences in the model. The authors perform a meta analysis over the different models and draw reccomendations for practice. They outline that SRL is a coherent framework for research in educational psychology and call for researchers and educators to draw from the current knowledge in SRL to enhance student’s learning and SRL skills.

blocks.text: Introduction

text.content: Introduction

rich_text.plain_text: Introduction

blocks.text: SRL has made substantial contributions to education psychology since its inception. One of the key conquests was the discrimination between SRL and metacognition. With time, the number of SRL models has been increasing and, in 2011, a handbook outlining the established methods of SRL was published. The authors propose to review the, at the time, existing knowledge on SRL.

text.content: SRL has made substantial contributions to education psychology since its inception. One of the key conquests was the discrimination between SRL and metacognition. The authors propose to review the, at the time, existing knowledge on SRL.

blocks.text: Methods

text.content: Methods

rich_text.plain_text: Methods

blocks.text: The criteria for inclusion in the work were only established SRL models that have been previously published and cited in peer reviewed papers. Models that did not fulfill these conditions were dismissed. The authors kept the Zimmerman, Boekaerts and Winne that were assumed to still be active - for example Borkowski’s model have not been considered.

blocks.text: Other models that have been used after 2001 were also considered for the literature, thus bringing the models used to be those by: Zimmerman, Pintrich, Boekarts, Winne, Hokfides and, finally, the model by Hadwin, Jarvela and Miller.

blocks.text: Model 1: Zimmerman - A socio-cognitive perspective of SRL Grounded by 3 Models

text.content: Model 1: Zimmerman - A socio-cognitive perspective of SRL Grounded by 3 Models

rich_text.plain_text: Model 1: Zimmerman - A socio-cognitive perspective of SRL Grounded by 3 Models

blocks.text: Zimmerman developed 3 distinct SRL models and started his approach using socio-cognitive theory: people acquire knowlesge from socialization and observation of others. The initial models was known as a Triadic Analysis of SRL that analyzed 3 forms of SRL - environment, behavior and personal level.

blocks.text: Foretought - where students preplan what they are about to do. It involves setting goals and developing a strategy to reach them.

text.content: Foretought - where students preplan what they are about to do. It involves setting goals and developing a strategy to reach them.

rich_text.plain_text: Foretought - where students preplan what they are about to do. It involves setting goals and developing a strategy to reach them.

blocks.text: Performance - In the performance phase, students are executing the task at hand. During this stage they are continuously monitoring their progress and using a number of self-control strategies to complete the task at hand.

blocks.text: Empyrical Evidence for the Cyclical Model

text.content: Empyrical Evidence for the Cyclical Model

rich_text.plain_text: Empyrical Evidence for the Cyclical Model

blocks.text: The cyclical model has been extensively tested in studies, both by Zimmerman and other authors. A study testing athletic skill profficiency between experts, non-experts and novices in multiple sports showcases that SRL skills play a role in the behaviour that is exhibited. In one example using basketball, experts performed more SRL related actions in comparison to others. In another example of Volleyball, SRL skills accounted for more than 90% of the explained variance in serving skills. Also in academic skills, higher achieving students exhibited an more SRL subprocesses than their colleagues. Keeping logs and diaries over progress has also been shown to positively affect SRL skills and performance.

text.content: The cyclical model has been extensively tested in studies, both by Zimmerman and other authors. blocks.text: Instruments and Measurement Methods

text.content: Instruments and Measurement Methods

rich_text.plain_text: Instruments and Measurement Methods

blocks.text: Zimmerman’s model measures subprocesses that are partly based from the Self-Regulated Learning Interview Schedule. Moreover, the author has also developed his own methods to assess SRL in experimental settings.  Another method was later developed by Magno using the Academic Self-Regulation Scale which has validated using correlation analysis with the results obtained from other models - more specifically the MSLQ and the Learning and Study Strategies Inventory.

blocks.text: According to the Boekarts model, students will be prone to adopt strategies that align with their emotional state (motivation). Students who see a task as threathning will shy away from it in order to maximize their well-being. On the other hand, if the tasks align with the student’s goals and needs, they will actively look to pursue development and acquire knowledge on their path to mastery.

blocks.text: Empyrical Evidence for the Dual Processing Model

text.content: Empyrical Evidence for the Dual Processing Model

rich_text.plain_text: Empyrical Evidence for the Dual Processing Model

blocks.text: The authors identified how cognitive appraisals determine emotion and learning intentions and, for example, found that different genders receive, emotionally, the appraisals, resulting into different responses. These results were validated in other studies, showing that, for example, boys and girls attend differently to some types of problems such as math: boys tend to be more positive and more willing to put the efforts whilst girls were less prone to engage. In addition, the authors showed that the interpretation of what is meant to be done in a learning activity are better explainers of learning intentions than other domain related measures such as ability or interest in the domain.

blocks.text: Success in mathematics homeworks under this model was also shown to be positively influence by value appraisals. In addition, the author managed to create neural networks (using 56 predictors) that achieved an accuracy ranging between 94 to 100% accuracy.

blocks.text: Metacognitively adapting studying - a phase where students reflect on their progress and decide to make long-term adaptations to their motivation, strategies and beliefs going forward. The model by Winne and Hadwin is less reliant on emotion than Boekaerts’s, instead focusing on goal-setting and the how students monitor their progress and the tasks they are performing.

blocks.text: Empirical Evidence for Winne and Hadwin’s model

text.content: Empirical Evidence for Winne and Hadwin’s model

rich_text.plain_text: Empirical Evidence for Winne and Hadwin’s model

blocks.text: While there is strong empyrical evidence for their work, there are some challenges lying in front of this model: the first is being more explicit on how phase 4 works - what processes are undertaken and what do the authors refer to as “long-term changes in their behavior”. Second - the model does not incorporate motivation. Thirdly, a discussion of how SRL evolves over the lifespan and finally, what is the impact of learning disabilities in SRL. More recent work has focused on applications of Data Mining and learning analytics to measure SRL. Up to this point, Winne’s model has been the only one explicitly focused on measurements of SRL using Trace data.

blocks.text: The Pintrich conceptual model includes metacognitive research such as perceptions of learning and of knowledge acquisition. Moreover, his work also shows that both regulation and affection can be regulated and argues that individuals can make attempts to control their own overt behavior. The final component is regulation of the learning context.

text.content: The Pintrich conceptual model includes metacognitive research such as perceptions of learning and of knowledge acquisition. Moreover, his work also shows that both regulation and affection can be regulated and argues that individuals can make attempts to control their own overt behavior. The final component is regulation of the learning context.

blocks.text: Efklides

text.content: Efklides

rich_text.plain_text: Efklides

blocks.text: Efklides has one of the strongest metacognition focus in comparison to other models (with the exception of Winne and Hadwin’s models). However, Efklides also puts a strong emphasis on motivation and affect. Despite it being fairly young (2011), the model has contributed to SRL research in great ways. The advantage of this model is that it builds upon existing SRL frameworks while also adding a connection with other metacognitive models.

blocks.text: The model has 2 levels:

text.content: The model has 2 levels:

rich_text.plain_text: The model has 2 levels:

blocks.text: The person level: this level refers to personal carachteristics of the students and comes into play when a task is viewed as resorting to memory knowledge, skills, motivational and metacognitive beliefs and affect. In the person level, the students play a more active role in determining what needs to be done and, in other words, are more in control.

text.content: The person level: this level refers to personal carachteristics of the students and comes into play when a task is viewed as resorting to memory knowledge, skills, motivational and metacognitive beliefs and affect. In the person level, the students play a more active role in determining what needs to be done and, in other words, are more in control.

rich_text.plain_text: The person level: this level refers to personal carachteristics of the students and comes into play when a task is viewed as resorting to memory knowledge, skills, motivational and metacognitive beliefs and affect. In the person level, the students play a more active role in determining what needs to be done and, in other words, are more in control.

blocks.text: The Task x Person level: This is a bottom-up level. In essence, metacognitivce activity takes control of the student’s actions with the focus on addressing the task-at-hand. In other words, the student dedicates the attention to minutia that are necessary to address the task at hand. At this level, addressing the task occupies most of the student’s attention.

blocks.text: Hadwin, Jarvela and Miller: SRL in collaborative learning

text.content: Hadwin, Jarvela and Miller: SRL in collaborative learning

rich_text.plain_text: Hadwin, Jarvela and Miller: SRL in collaborative learning

blocks.text: This model seems to be focused in describing how SRL comes into play in the context of collaborative work. The model draws from Winne and Hadwin’s model and tries to complement it with the introduction of other SRL constructs in a group context.
